Category: --- SITE LEVEL: project boundary and major divisions of the construction site ---

Description: The overall geographic boundary of a construction project — everything within the property line or limit of work. Top of the location hierarchy; every project has exactly one. Anchored by coordinates (lat/long or state plane). Contains all buildings, exterior zones, staging areas, and infrastructure. Project-level records attach here: daily reports, weather logs, safety metrics, site logistics.
Industry Standard: OmniClass Table 13; COBie Facility
Common Values: Single campus, city block, parcel, right-of-way corridor, multi-building campus
Source Systems: Procore; BIM 360; PlanGrid; P6; CMMS (Archibus/Maximo); GIS platforms
Procore Coverage: High — projects table with location data and coordinates; all project-level records attach here
Benchmarking Use: Site-level metrics anchor all cross-project comparison — defect density per project and cost per SF of site
Notes: Multi-building campuses are one site. The site boundary defines geographic extent. Level 0 in the hierarchy.

Description: A distinct structure within the project site — the primary vertical container for constructed space. Multi-building projects separate records by structure. Single-building projects still carry one Building node for hierarchy consistency. Children: Floor/Level, Core/Shaft. Systems: structural frame, exterior enclosure, roofing, elevators, building-level HVAC and electrical.
Industry Standard: OmniClass Table 13; Uniformat II Level 1; IBC Chapter 5 (building classification)
Common Values: Building A/B, North Tower, South Tower, Parking Structure, Central Plant, Data Hall
Source Systems: Procore; Revit; BIM 360; PlanGrid; Bluebeam; P6; Sage 300
Procore Coverage: High — locations table supports building as a hierarchy level; observations, punch items, and inspections reference location_id
Benchmarking Use: Compare defect rates and schedule per building within multi-building projects
Notes: Building footprint defines horizontal boundary. Level 1 in hierarchy.

Description: A defined area outside the building envelope but within the project site — organized by function or orientation. Facade orientation zones (N/S/E/W) are critical for enclosure work as different exposures have different weather and solar conditions. Children: Sub-Zone (optional). Systems: site & earthwork, site utilities, site improvements, exterior enclosure (by facade orientation).
Industry Standard: OmniClass Table 13; MasterFormat 32 00 00 (Exterior Improvements)
Common Values: North/South/East/West Facade, Courtyard, Plaza, Loading Dock, Parking Lot, Green Space, Entrance Drive
Source Systems: Procore; Revit; BIM 360; PlanGrid; Bluebeam; P6; landscape architecture tools
Procore Coverage: Medium — locations table supports exterior zones but adoption varies; field records reference exterior locations
Benchmarking Use: Compare site improvement defect rates and cost by zone type across projects
Notes: Facade orientation zones connect enclosure scope to physical location. Level 1 in hierarchy.

Description: Designated areas for material storage, prefabrication, and equipment staging. Shift as construction progresses — what was steel staging becomes curtain wall staging. Tight urban sites have minimal laydown, requiring just-in-time delivery. Leaf node. Records: delivery logs, material tracking, equipment logs, safety observations for material handling.
Industry Standard: OSHA 1926.250 (Materials Storage); site logistics planning standards
Common Values: Main Laydown Yard, Steel Staging, Curtain Wall Staging, Dumpster Area, Concrete Washout, Crane Pad, Trailer Compound
Source Systems: Procore; site logistics software; P6; delivery management platforms
Procore Coverage: Medium — locations table supports but rarely used for staging; daily logs capture activity
Benchmarking Use: Compare laydown area efficiency (deliveries per SF of staging) by site type
Notes: Laydown areas are dynamic and shift through the project. Urban vs. suburban site constraints drive logistics strategy. Level 1 leaf node.

Description: Public or utility easement areas where project work occurs outside the property line. Governed by municipal permits with strict restoration requirements. Work windows may be restricted (night work, traffic hours). Separate inspection jurisdiction from building department. Leaf node.
Industry Standard: MUTCD (Manual on Uniform Traffic Control Devices); municipal ROW permits
Common Values: Street Cut (Water Main), Sidewalk Reconstruction, Utility Ductbank, Curb Cut, Traffic Signal Work
Source Systems: Procore; municipal permit systems; P6; utility coordination systems
Procore Coverage: Low — not specifically modeled; daily logs and permits capture ROW work
Benchmarking Use: Compare ROW work duration and restoration cost by municipality
Notes: ROW work adds schedule risk from municipal coordination. Common on urban and infrastructure projects. Level 1 leaf node.

Description: Contractor-provided temporary structures that support construction but are removed at completion. General conditions cost items. Location on site affects logistics. Removed before final site improvements. Leaf node. Records: setup/removal logs, equipment logs, safety inspections, temporary utility records.
Industry Standard: OSHA 1926 Subpart F (Fire Protection); temporary utility standards
Common Values: Job Trailer/Field Office, Temporary Parking, Guard Shack, Decontamination Unit, Temporary Restrooms, Temporary Power Distribution
Source Systems: Procore; equipment management software; P6; general conditions tracking
Procore Coverage: Low — not specifically modeled as locations; daily logs and equipment records capture
Benchmarking Use: Compare temporary facility cost as % of general conditions by project type and duration
Notes: Temporary facilities are general conditions items not part of the permanent building. Level 1 leaf node.

Category: --- BUILDING VERTICAL: floor-level organization within a structure ---

Description: A horizontal plane within a building that divides it vertically — the primary vertical organizing unit. The most universally used location level. Most field records attach at floor level or below. Below-grade levels have different system profiles (waterproofing, underslab MEP). Every building system has work organized by floor. Children: Zone/Wing, Room/Space, Area, Ceiling Plenum.
Industry Standard: OmniClass Table 13; COBie Floor; IBC Chapter 5 (stories)
Common Values: Basement 2 (B2), Basement 1 (B1), Ground Floor (L1), Level 2-N, Mezzanine, Penthouse, Roof
Source Systems: Procore; Revit; BIM 360; PlanGrid; Bluebeam; P6; Sage 300
Procore Coverage: High — locations table supports floors as primary hierarchy level; most field records attach at floor level
Benchmarking Use: Compare defect density per floor and floor completion rates across projects
Notes: The universal organizing unit for field work. Level 2 in hierarchy.

Description: Floors below ground level — basements, sub-basements, parking levels, underground mechanical spaces. Unique challenges: waterproofing, hydrostatic pressure, radon mitigation, garage ventilation, fire department access. Waterproofing defects are among the most expensive to remediate. Children: Zone/Wing, Room/Space, Area.
Industry Standard: IBC Chapter 4 (special uses); ACI 350 (water-retaining structures); NFPA 88A (parking structures)
Common Values: B1, B2, B3, Sub-Basement, Underground Parking P1/P2/P3
Source Systems: Procore; Revit; BIM 360; PlanGrid; Bluebeam; P6
Procore Coverage: High — modeled as floor levels in locations table; observations capture waterproofing and ventilation issues
Benchmarking Use: Compare below-grade waterproofing defect rates and cost per below-grade SF
Notes: Below-grade levels have distinct system profiles from above-grade. Waterproofing failures are a top rework driver. Level 2 in hierarchy.

Description: The top of the building including roof surface, mechanical penthouse, and rooftop equipment areas. Both an enclosure system (membrane, insulation, flashing) and a mechanical platform (RTUs, cooling towers, exhaust fans). Safety-critical for fall protection. Access logistics affect equipment replacement in operations. Children: Rooftop Zone (optional).
Industry Standard: NRCA Roofing Manual; OSHA 1926.501 (Fall Protection); ASHRAE (rooftop equipment)
Common Values: Main Roof, Low Roof, Mechanical Penthouse, Roof Terrace, Green Roof, Cooling Tower Platform
Source Systems: Procore; Revit; BIM 360; PlanGrid; Bluebeam; P6; roofing warranty systems
Procore Coverage: High — modeled as a floor level; roofing observations and equipment inspections attach here
Benchmarking Use: Compare roofing defect rates and rooftop equipment cost per building SF
Notes: Dual function — enclosure and mechanical platform. Fall protection observations are a leading safety signal. Level 2 in hierarchy.

Category: --- FLOOR DIVISION: zones and vertical elements within a floor ---

Description: A horizontal subdivision of a floor — used for phasing, scope separation, or building geometry. Project-specific designations defined by the team to organize work. A 50-story tower might define zones as quadrants per floor. A hospital might define zones as departments. Drives the 3-week lookahead schedule. Children: Room/Space, Area.
Industry Standard: Project-specific; AIA A201 phasing; lean construction zone planning
Common Values: North/South/East/West Wing, Zone A/B/C, Phase 1/2 Area, Tower vs. Podium, Department
Source Systems: Procore; Revit; BIM 360; PlanGrid; P6; lean scheduling tools
Procore Coverage: Medium — locations table supports zones but not all projects use this level; field records reference zones inconsistently
Benchmarking Use: Compare defect density by zone type and phase completion rates
Notes: Zones are project-specific — no universal standard. Level 3 in hierarchy.

Description: Vertical penetrations that run through multiple floors — stairs, elevators, mechanical shafts, duct risers. Unique vertical elements that span the building height. Firestopping at every floor penetration is a code requirement and a major quality/punch item generator. Elevator hoistways have specific inspection requirements. Spans multiple floors rather than sitting within one.
Industry Standard: IBC Chapter 7 (fire-resistance-rated assemblies); NFPA 101 (shafts); ASME A17.1 (elevator hoistways)
Common Values: Stair A/B, Elevator Shaft 1-4, Mechanical Shaft, Electrical Riser, Plumbing Riser, Duct Shaft
Source Systems: Procore; Revit; BIM 360; PlanGrid; Bluebeam; elevator inspection systems
Procore Coverage: Medium — can be modeled in locations but often not separated; firestopping observations track shaft-level issues
Benchmarking Use: Compare firestopping deficiency rates per shaft count and stair completion rates
Notes: Vertical elements that span multiple floors — unique in the hierarchy. Firestopping is one of the most inspected items. Level 3.

Description: The space between the finished ceiling and the structural deck above — where most MEP distribution occurs. The most contested space in a building — 5+ trades working in the same 18-36 inch space. Above-ceiling inspection before drywall/ceiling close is the most critical quality hold point. Healthcare interstitial spaces are full-height walk-through plenums.
Industry Standard: IBC 717 (fire dampers in plenums); NFPA 90A (plenum-rated materials); ASHRAE (return air plenums)
Common Values: Typical Ceiling Plenum (per floor/zone), Return Air Plenum, Interstitial Space (healthcare)
Source Systems: Procore; Revit; Navisworks; BIM 360; PlanGrid; Bluebeam
Procore Coverage: Low — not typically modeled as a separate location; coordination issues and observations reference plenums in free text
Benchmarking Use: Compare above-ceiling deficiency rates per floor as a proxy for MEP coordination quality
Notes: Above-ceiling inspection is the most critical quality hold point. Clash detection results often reference plenum space. Level 3 in hierarchy.

Category: --- ROOM & SPACE: individually identifiable enclosed spaces ---

Description: An individually identifiable enclosed space within a building — defined by walls, doors, and a room number. The unit of occupancy. Healthcare patient rooms may have 150+ punch items each. Hotel rooms are repetitive — one model room sets the standard. Room numbering convention varies by project. BIM models room data. Leaf node for most purposes.
Industry Standard: OmniClass Table 13; COBie Space; ADA/ICC A117.1 (accessibility requirements per room type)
Common Values: Office 301, Conference Room 205, Patient Room 412, Classroom 103, Hotel Room 1205, Apartment 8B, Lab 502
Source Systems: Procore; Revit; BIM 360; PlanGrid; Bluebeam; CMMS (Archibus/Maximo); room scheduling
Procore Coverage: Medium — locations table supports room-level but adoption decreases at this depth; punch items commonly reference rooms
Benchmarking Use: Compare punch items per room type and finish quality defect rates across projects
Notes: Room-level tracking requires BIM or disciplined naming. Rooms bridge construction and operations. Level 4 in hierarchy.

Description: A space defined by its function rather than enclosure — may span rooms or be part of a larger space. Functional areas have specific code requirements: mechanical rooms (ventilation, fire separation), electrical rooms (clearances, access), data rooms (cooling, power), kitchens (grease duct, hood suppression, health department). These drive inspections. Leaf node.
Industry Standard: IBC Chapter 4 (special uses by function); NFPA 70 (electrical rooms); NFPA 96 (commercial kitchens)
Common Values: Lobby, Corridor, Elevator Lobby, Mechanical Room, Electrical Room, IDF/MDF, Kitchen, Server Room, Mail Room
Source Systems: Procore; Revit; BIM 360; PlanGrid; Bluebeam; CMMS; health/safety inspection systems
Procore Coverage: Medium — supported in locations but requires disciplined setup; inspections reference functional areas
Benchmarking Use: Compare deficiency rates by functional area type (mechanical rooms vs. corridors vs. kitchens)
Notes: Code-driven spaces with specific inspection requirements regardless of project type. Level 4 in hierarchy.

Description: Spaces with plumbing fixtures and waterproofing requirements — bathrooms, kitchens, janitor closets, mechanical rooms with floor drains. Waterproofing testing (shower pan flood tests, floor drain tests) required before finishes. Plumbing fixture rough-in must align with tile layout. ADA compliance heavily inspected in restrooms. Healthcare wet areas have infection control requirements. Leaf node.
Industry Standard: IPC/UPC (plumbing codes); ADA/ICC A117.1 (accessible fixtures); ANSI A108 (tile installation)
Common Values: Restroom M/F, Shower Room, Locker Room, Kitchen, Janitor Closet, Pool Area
Source Systems: Procore; Revit; BIM 360; PlanGrid; Bluebeam; plumbing inspection systems
Procore Coverage: Medium — tracked as rooms in locations; waterproofing observations and plumbing inspections reference wet areas
Benchmarking Use: Compare waterproofing defect rates per wet area count and plumbing punch items per fixture
Notes: Waterproofing failures in wet areas are a major rework driver. ADA compliance is a primary inspection focus. Level 4 in hierarchy.

Category: --- SUB-SPACE: granular locations within a room or space (BIM-driven) ---

Description: An individual wall or surface within a room — identified by orientation or designation. Primarily BIM-driven and punch-item-driven. In high-volume environments (hotels, residential), tracking deficiencies by wall enables pattern analysis (e.g., north walls consistently have drywall cracking from thermal movement). Leaf node.
Industry Standard: OmniClass Table 13; GA-216 (drywall finish levels); ASTM C840
Common Values: North/South/East/West Wall, Column Line A, Soffit, Bulkhead
Source Systems: Revit; BIM 360; PlanGrid; Procore; Bluebeam
Procore Coverage: Low — not modeled in locations; punch items may reference walls in free text descriptions
Benchmarking Use: Compare surface deficiency patterns by orientation and finish type across high-volume projects
Notes: Wall-level tracking requires BIM integration. Most valuable in repetitive programs (hotels, residential, healthcare). Level 5+ in hierarchy.

Description: The specific location of a major piece of installed equipment — tracked for commissioning, maintenance, and warranty. Every major equipment piece gets a unique tag (AHU-1, CHL-1) that persists from design through 30+ years of operations. Equipment schedules link tag to location to specifications to O&M manuals. Bridges construction and operations. Leaf node.
Industry Standard: ASHRAE Standard 202 (commissioning); COBie (equipment handover); NFPA 70 (equipment identification)
Common Values: AHU-1 (Mech Room B1), Chiller-1 (Central Plant), Panel LP-3A (Elec Room L2), VAV-3-01 (Plenum L3)
Source Systems: Revit; BIM 360; Procore; CMMS (Maximo/Archibus); commissioning tools (CxAlloy)
Procore Coverage: Low — equipment not modeled as locations; commissioning records and O&M link equipment to spaces
Benchmarking Use: Compare equipment commissioning defect rates and warranty claim rates by equipment type
Notes: Equipment tags bridge construction and operations lifecycle. Critical for facility management handover. Level 5+ in hierarchy.

Description: A specific penetration through a fire-rated or smoke-rated assembly — tracked for firestopping compliance. Every penetration through a rated assembly must be firestopped per a UL-listed system. Tracking individual penetrations enables deficiency pattern analysis. Increasingly required by special inspection programs. One of the most inspected and most deficient items in construction. Leaf node.
Industry Standard: IBC 714 (penetrations); UL 1479/UL 2079 (fire test standards); ASTM E814
Common Values: Electrical conduit through 2-hr wall, Plumbing riser sleeve, Duct penetration at stair wall
Source Systems: Revit; Navisworks; BIM 360; Procore; Bluebeam; firestop inspection software
Procore Coverage: Low — not modeled as locations; firestopping observations and special inspections reference penetrations in description
Benchmarking Use: Compare firestopping deficiency rates per penetration count and per rated assembly area
Notes: Firestopping is one of the most inspected and most deficient items across all project types. Level 5+ in hierarchy.

Category: --- CROSS-CUTTING OVERLAYS: location concepts that span the physical hierarchy ---

Description: A code-defined area enclosed by fire-rated or smoke-rated construction — may span rooms or zones. A regulatory overlay on the physical layout that defines where fire barriers must be maintained, smoke dampers required, and rated doors installed. Healthcare smoke compartments drive corridor separation and are a primary inspection focus. Contains rooms/spaces within the compartment.
Industry Standard: IBC Chapters 7 & 7A; NFPA 101 (Life Safety Code); NFPA 80 (fire doors); NFPA 105 (smoke doors)
Common Values: Smoke Compartment A (L2 North), Fire Area 1, Fire Barrier at Grid 5, Horizontal Exit
Source Systems: Revit; BIM 360; Procore; Bluebeam; fire marshal inspection systems
Procore Coverage: Low — not modeled as locations; fire/life safety inspections and observations reference compartments
Benchmarking Use: Compare fire barrier deficiency rates per compartment count and by building occupancy type
Notes: Fire compartments are a regulatory overlay not a physical construction element. Cross-cutting — spans multiple hierarchy levels.

Description: A temporary designation that defines where active construction work is occurring — shifts as the project progresses. Dynamic — moves weekly or daily. Safety zones (crane radius, excavation, hot work) have specific regulatory requirements. Healthcare occupied renovations require infection control risk assessment (ICRA) zones with negative pressure and barriers. Temporary overlay on any hierarchy level.
Industry Standard: OSHA 1926 Subpart N (Cranes); OSHA 29 CFR 1926.352 (Hot Work); ICRA (healthcare)
Common Values: Active Pour Zone, Steel Erection Zone, Tower Crane Radius, Hot Work Area, Dust Control Zone, ICRA Zone
Source Systems: Procore; site logistics software; P6; safety management platforms
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily logs and safety observations capture work zones; not modeled in location hierarchy
Benchmarking Use: Compare work zone incident rates and phase completion variance across projects
Notes: Work zones are dynamic and temporary — they don't persist in the location hierarchy. Healthcare ICRA zones are heavily regulated.

Description: A grouping of spaces for the purpose of systems commissioning — may align with HVAC zones, floors, or functional groups. Groups equipment and spaces tested together. May cross floor boundaries (a single AHU serving floors 1-5 creates one zone spanning 5 floor locations). Organized by mechanical system boundaries not physical construction.
Industry Standard: ASHRAE Standard 202 (Commissioning); ASHRAE Guideline 0; LEED EAp1/EAc3
Common Values: CxZone 1 (L1-L5 East), CxZone 2 (L1-L5 West), CxZone 3 (Penthouse Mech), CxZone 4 (Central Plant)
Source Systems: Commissioning tools (CxAlloy); Procore; BIM 360; Revit; BAS systems
Procore Coverage: Low — not modeled in locations; commissioning records may reference zones in free text
Benchmarking Use: Compare commissioning defect rates per zone and functional test pass rates by system type
Notes: Commissioning zones bridge location and system — organized by mechanical system boundaries. Cross-cutting.

Description: A coordinate location defined by the intersection of structural grid lines — the universal reference system for locating anything in a building. Every drawing references grid lines. Survey and layout is done to grid lines. Equipment locations, column locations, and major building elements are described by grid intersection. The bridge between 2D drawings and 3D physical space. Reference point at all floors.
Industry Standard: AIA A201 (drawing standards); structural grid conventions; survey control standards
Common Values: Grid A/1, Grid B/3, Grid J/12, Column Line intersections
Source Systems: Revit; AutoCAD; Navisworks; survey instruments; Procore; PlanGrid; Bluebeam
Procore Coverage: Low — not explicitly modeled; drawings reference grid lines; observations and RFIs reference grids in free text
Benchmarking Use: Grid-referenced data enables spatial density analysis (defects per grid bay) on BIM-enabled projects
Notes: Grid lines are the universal coordinate system of construction. Cross-cutting — applies at all hierarchy levels.

Category: --- STANDARD MEASURES: KPIs derived from location hierarchy data ---

Description: Measures how many levels of the location hierarchy a company uses in field records — from site-level only (depth 1) to room-level (depth 4+). Higher depth = more granular defect tracking. Calculation: max hierarchy depth with >10 records attached / total available levels. Source: field records linked to location hierarchy.
Industry Standard: None — operational maturity metric
Common Values: Depth 1 (site only), Depth 2 (floor level), Depth 3 (zone level), Depth 4+ (room level)
Source Systems: Procore; PlanGrid; BIM 360
Procore Coverage: High — derived from location hierarchy and record attachment
Benchmarking Use: Compare location tracking maturity across companies and project types
Notes: Low depth often indicates location setup was incomplete not that the project was simple.

Description: Count of quality observations, punch items, and inspection deficiencies per location node — reveals where problems concentrate. Calculation: (observations + punch items + deficient inspections) / location count at each hierarchy level. Normalized per floor, per room, or per SF.
Industry Standard: ISO 9001 (nonconformance tracking); Lean Construction (defect mapping)
Common Values: 5-15 punch items per room (typical), 20-50 per floor (commercial), 100+ per floor (healthcare)
Source Systems: Procore; PlanGrid; BIM 360; Bluebeam
Procore Coverage: High — observations, punch items, and inspections all reference location_id
Benchmarking Use: Compare defect density by location type across project types and building uses
Notes: Normalize by location count or area to enable cross-project comparison.

Description: Count of punch list items assigned to each room-level location — the primary closeout workload metric. Calculation: total punch items / total rooms. Segmented by room type for cross-project comparison. High-finish rooms (healthcare, hospitality) generate more punch items.
Industry Standard: AIA/AGC best practices for punchlist management
Common Values: 3-8 per room (standard commercial), 10-20 per room (healthcare), 15-30 per room (hospitality)
Source Systems: Procore; PlanGrid; BIM 360; Bluebeam
Procore Coverage: High — punch items reference location_id; room-level location setup required
Benchmarking Use: Compare closeout workload per room across building types and quality standards
Notes: Requires room-level location setup. Projects without room locations can't benchmark at this level.

Description: Percentage of punch items and observations closed per floor over time — tracks vertical closeout progress. Calculation: closed_items / total_items per floor measured weekly. Reveals which floors are lagging and whether closeout follows a logical sequence (top-down or bottom-up).
Industry Standard: Lean Construction (last planner system); AIA closeout procedures
Common Values: 0-100% per floor; typical closeout velocity 1-2 floors per week
Source Systems: Procore; PlanGrid; BIM 360
Procore Coverage: High — derived from punch items and observations with floor-level locations
Benchmarking Use: Compare closeout velocity (floors per week) across similar building types
Notes: Requires floor-level location setup. Sequence analysis reveals whether closeout follows schedule logic.

Description: Percentage of defined locations that have at least one quality record (observation, inspection, or punch item) attached — measures inspection thoroughness. Calculation: locations_with_records / total_defined_locations. Low coverage suggests incomplete inspections.
Industry Standard: ISO 19011 (audit sampling); quality management sampling standards
Common Values: 60-80% typical; 90%+ for commissioning; <50% suggests gaps
Source Systems: Procore; PlanGrid; BIM 360
Procore Coverage: High — derived from location hierarchy and record attachment
Benchmarking Use: Compare inspection thoroughness across projects and companies
Notes: Meaningful only when location hierarchy is fully set up. Low coverage is an actionable signal for quality managers.

Description: Count of safety observations and incidents per work zone or floor — identifies high-risk areas. Calculation: safety_observations / active_work_zones. Tracks whether safety issues cluster in specific areas (elevated work, confined spaces, active excavation).
Industry Standard: OSHA recordkeeping; ANSI Z10 (OHS Management); site-specific safety plans
Common Values: 0-5 per zone per week (typical); >10 indicates elevated risk area
Source Systems: Procore; safety management platforms; P6; daily log systems
Procore Coverage: Medium — safety observations reference location_id but work zones are captured in free text
Benchmarking Use: Compare safety observation density by location type and work phase
Notes: Cross-domain with Safety. Location-based safety analysis requires location_id on safety observations.

Description: Percentage of inspected penetrations with firestopping deficiencies — a top quality metric for multi-story buildings. Calculation: deficient_penetrations / inspected_penetrations. Segmented by shaft type, rated assembly type, and floor. One of the most inspected items in construction.
Industry Standard: IBC 714; UL 1479/UL 2079; special inspection requirements
Common Values: 5-15% first-pass deficiency rate (typical); <5% indicates strong quality program
Source Systems: Procore; firestop inspection software; Bluebeam; special inspection reports
Procore Coverage: Medium — observations and inspections capture firestopping issues but penetration-level tracking requires BIM
Benchmarking Use: Compare firestopping deficiency rates across building types and inspection programs
Notes: Cross-domain with Building Elements (firestopping) and Quality (inspection deficiency). Highly regulated.
